data.title: GE Vernova: All Ready For The Energy Revolution
data.description:
Why GE Vernova?

GE Vernova (NYSE:GEV) is one of the best positioned companies to benefit from the growing global demand for electricity, especially given the boom in AI-powered data centers and the need for modern, efficient networks. The large backlog provides excellent revenue visibility for the next few years, giving investors confidence in its long-term growth prospects.

However, while GEV's fundamentals are compelling and the company is improving its margins and cash flow, I believe much of this potential is already priced into the stock at current levels. The premium valuation reflects high expectations for flawless execution and continued momentum in turbine prices and new orders. In the absence of further margin expansion or exceptional new contracts, the upside potential appears limited, making patience or waiting for a better entry point the most prudent approach at this time.

I am also convinced that the company will continue to grow, and I am confident in an improvement in margins, but in an objective analysis we must put aside hopes to make room for the numbers and to date, the financial data speak clearly.

Company Overview

GE Vernova is a division of General Electric. Its mission is to decarbonize the world and make electricity more reliable, sustainable and affordable. The company focuses on the global energy transition, which is the shift from fossil fuels to cleaner energy such as highly efficient gas, renewables and advanced grid solutions.

GE Vernova operates in three main areas:

  1. Power – This division produces large-scale gas turbines, used to generate electricity in traditional power plants. The company also offers long-term maintenance services, a source of recurring revenue.
  2. Wind – This includes both onshore and offshore wind businesses. GE Vernova produces wind blades and components through LM Wind Power, one of the world's leading companies in this sector. Wind, although less profitable due to high costs, is a strategic area.
  3. Electrification – This division deals with everything needed to get electricity from the point of generation to homes and businesses. We are talking about transformers, high voltage systems (HVDC), switchgear, and grid management software. Electrification is crucial to integrating renewables and making grids more stable and safe, especially as the use of technologies such as data centers and electric vehicle charging increases.

Backlog

GE Vernova’s backlog, or the total value of contracts signed but still to be delivered, has reached over $123 billion at the beginning of 2025. A frightening number, if you think about it. This means that the company already has guaranteed orders and revenues to be realized in the coming years, giving investors visibility on future earnings.

Corporate Vision

GE Vernova states on its official website that it wants to be the technology partner that allows to reduce CO₂ emissions, building a more sustainable and equitable energy future. The company estimates that huge investments in electrical infrastructure and more efficient power plants will be needed in the coming decades to support the growing demand for electricity globally.

Financial Performance

It is good to start with an overview of the last earnings season. In the first quarter of 2025, GE Vernova showed significant growth in almost all key indicators:

  • Orders: $10.2 billion, up 8% organically compared to the same quarter last year. This is important because it indicates how many new orders are coming in: more orders mean future revenue.
  • Revenue: $8.0 billion, up 15% organically. Organic growth excludes the effect of foreign exchange or acquisitions/sales of assets, so it truly reflects the improvement of the existing business.
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin: 5.7%, more than double from 2.6% in 1Q 2024. a figure that shows that the company is improving operational efficiency and is able to retain a higher share of revenues as operating profit.
  • Free cash flow (FCF): $975 million, with a conversion to net income of 369%: this means that GEV generated much more cash than what was shown by the accounting net profit, a very positive sign for financial health.

Let's open the hood and take a closer look at the various segments:

  1. Power: revenues up 16%, with EBITDA margin up to 11.5%. The increase is mainly linked to strong demand for gas turbines, improved average prices per kW and greater profitability of contracts.
  2. Electrification: even better performance, with revenues up 18% and EBITDA margin up from 4% to 11.4%. This confirms the trend of growing demand for network equipment and HVDC systems, also driven by investments in data centers.
  3. Wind: mixed signal. Revenues grew by 15%, but the EBITDA margin remained negative at -7.9%. This shows that despite the volumes, the issues related to costs and logistics of wind persist, especially in offshore.

These results clearly show that GEV is well positioned to take advantage of the growing demand for energy and electricity infrastructure, with an impressive improvement in profitability. However, the Wind segment remains a weak point that could continue to weigh on overall margins, especially if the cost dynamics in offshore and the complexity of supply chains do not stabilize. A common theme for almost all companies in this business, after all.

Let's focus on the clear improvement in free cash flow: for an industrial company, generating cash consistently is an indicator of solidity. If GEV can further improve Wind profitability, the potential for further expansion of overall margins is significant.

Price Target & Comparables

According to Bloomberg estimates, GE Vernova is now trading at around 28x EV/EBITDA on 2026 estimates, significantly higher than peers such as Eaton, Siemens Energy and Fuji Electric, which on average trade at 15–25x. This premium reflects GEV’s outstanding visibility into future revenues and its unique position in the global energy transition, but it also means the company will need to maintain excellent operating performance to justify these levels.

From the guidance and segment trends we estimate for 2026:

  • Expected 2026 revenues = USD 40.4 billion
  • Expected EBITDA margin = 11.4%
  • Estimated 2026 EBITDA = USD 40.4 × USD 0.114 = USD 4.6 billion

To calculate a conservative fair value, we apply a target multiple of 25x:

  • EV fair value = 4.6 × 25 = 115 billion USD

Add the net cash position, estimated at approximately 8 billion USD:

  • Equity fair value = 115 + 8 = 123 billion USD

Estimating 273 million diluted shares:

  • Target price = 123 billion USD / 273 million shares = 450 USD/share

I therefore believe that a target price of USD 450 already largely reflects the growth potential resulting from the current record backlog and the expected improvement in margins in the coming years. However, I consider it possible that the stock will reach higher valuations only under specific conditions:

  • a further sustainable increase in margins, especially in the Power and Electrification segments, which are already showing positive trends but which should expand well beyond the 11–12% forecast for 2026;
  • a structural improvement in the Wind segment, which to date continues to record operating losses and represents a drag on the group's overall profitability;
  • the arrival of new contracts of exceptional size, such as orders related to the Small Modular Reactors (SMR) projects or large data center infrastructure investments, which could substantially increase the backlog already above 120 billion USD and bring further visibility on revenues and margins after 2028.

In a scenario where these factors materialize, I believe a much higher target price is plausible, consistent with valuations above USD 500 per share, thanks to:

  • Potential fair value EV = 4.6 bn EBITDA × 30 (highest multiple) = 138 bn USD
  • Potential equity fair value = 138 + 8 = 146 bn USD
  • Potential target price = 146 bn USD / 273 million shares = 535 USD/share

The 30x EV/EBITDA multiple is my guess, based on GEV's current valuation and strong growth scenarios, as has historically occurred in similar companies.

However, in the absence of these developments, I believe that the current valuation already incorporates most of the growth expectations. The analysis of the official documents available in the GE Vernova Investor Relations section confirms how the challenges in the Wind segment and the margins still lower than competitors make operational execution a decisive factor for the sustainability of such high multiples.

Personally, I consider GE Vernova an interesting long-term opportunity for those who want to expose themselves to the electrification and decarbonization megatrends, but I believe that at current prices the risk/return profile is unbalanced: I see new entries justified only in the event of a correction that brings the stock back to multiples more in line with the sector fundamentals or if the company manages to surprise the market with above-expected results.

However, in the absence of these developments, I believe that the current valuation already incorporates most of the growth expectations. Personally, I consider GE Vernova an interesting long-term opportunity for those who want to expose themselves to the electrification and decarbonization megatrends, but the current prices would seem right. I see new entries justified only in the event of a correction that brings the stock back to multiples more in line with the sector fundamentals, or if the company manages to surprise the market with above-expected results.

Risks

Despite the excellent long-term prospects, I see several risk factors that could weigh on GE Vernova's performance and the sustainability of its current multiples:

  1. Margins still volatile in Wind. The Wind segment continues to record significant operating losses (EBITDA margin still negative at -7.9% in 1Q'25). Without a structural improvement, wind risks remaining a drag on overall profitability
  2. Dependence on the industrial investment cycle and data centers. A large part of the growth expected by GEV is based on demand for new power plants, network infrastructure and hyperscale data centers. A slowdown in investments in these sectors could reduce new orders or lead to postponements, eroding visibility on revenues.
  3. Geopolitical and regulatory risks. GE Vernova has significant exposure to international markets and projects subject to permits and regulations (e.g. nuclear, large grid infrastructure). Regulatory changes, trade tensions (such as US-China tariffs) or political instability in key areas could delay projects or increase costs.
  4. Tight Valuation. The stock trades at multiples well above the industry average. This means that even small disappointments on margins, backlog or future guidance could trigger significant corrections, increasing volatility for shareholders.

Opportunities

Despite the risks, I see several factors that could be significant catalysts for GE Vernova in the coming years:

  1. Structural demand for power capacity: the global energy transition and electrification of the economy require huge investments in new power plants, high-efficiency turbines and more robust grids. GEV is positioned at the forefront.
  2. Rising gas turbine pricing: average gas turbine prices have more than doubled in recent years (from around $1,200–$1,400/kW to ~$2,400/kW, source: Bloomberg Intelligence). If this momentum continues, as the current level of demand suggests, GEV could benefit from further margin expansion in the Power and Electrification segments.
  3. Small Modular Reactor Development: GE Vernova is among the companies best positioned to benefit from the growing interest in modular nuclear reactors. The potential acquisition of SMR contracts could open a new source of multi-year revenues starting in 2029, further expanding the backlog and diversifying the portfolio.
  4. Maintenance: gas turbines tend to generate a strong increase in service revenues when they enter the maintenance phase, typically 3–4 years after delivery

My take

I believe that GE Vernova is one of the best positioned companies to take advantage of the growing global demand for electricity and the energy transition, thanks to a record backlog and progressive improvement in margins. For me, it is a stock to keep in the portfolio to take advantage of all the possible catalysts listed above. On the other hand, I believe that at current prices, much of this prospect is already priced into the stock: the valuation already incorporates very aggressive expectations on improving profitability, especially in the Wind segment.

For this reason, I consider the stock attractive for the long term, but I think it is more prudent to wait for a possible correction or concrete signs of improvement in margins before entering. In a scenario of margins above current guidance and significant new orders, I still see room for a target price well above $500 per share.

In light of these factors, my rating is Hold, waiting for operational confirmations or a more interesting entry point.

data.title: BITO: On The Verge Of A Break To New Highs
data.description:
As alt-coin OG Bitcoin is knocking on the door to all-time highs once again, it’s a good chance to review which funds are best to gain exposure, if you so choose. Cryptos are firmly in the mainstream of investing now, as the rise of exchange-traded funds in their dozens, making it easier than ever to gain exposure to Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies.

One very popular choice for Bitcoin exposure is the ProShares Bitcoin ETF (BITO), a $2.5 billion fund that tracks the price of Bitcoin, but with a twist. The last time I covered BITO was last October, and I called it a strong buy on the idea that its downtrend was ending. It’s up 61% on a total return basis since then, so we’ll call that one a win. But what about now? I’m still quite bullish, and I’m doubling down on my strong buy rating.

What is BITO?

BITO seeks to track the performance of Bitcoin over time, minus fees and expenses. That’s a familiar goal as there are numerous funds that look to do the very same thing. But rather than owning Bitcoin directly, BITO owns a huge position in Bitcoin futures. Here’s what the fund owns as of the close on Friday.

The fund owns $2.5 billion in July Bitcoin futures, and it rolls its positions regularly so it’s always got what is essentially spot exposure to Bitcoin futures. That, in turn, tracks spot Bitcoin prices without owning Bitcoin directly. This is a slightly different philosophy to funds that directly own Bitcoin, but as we’ll see below, owning futures works as well.

This is the 20-day rolling correlation between BITO and Bitcoin itself, and its current value is 0.89. That’s actually a bit lower than it has been in recent months, when it spent a lot of time very near 1.00. At any rate, 0.89 is still almost perfect, so if you’re looking for Bitcoin exposure, you can do much worse.

The other thing is that BITO is not just a way to gain exposure to Bitcoin pricing; it offers massive distributions as well. Some funds do not offer distributions, and therefore, their price charts will more closely reflect the movements of Bitcoin itself. BITO offers that exposure to Bitcoin, but also a huge amount of distributions.

ProShares has an explanation of how BITO (and its other similar funds) generate distribution, if you’re curious. And those distributions are extremely consequential to total returns for BITO.

We can see that distributions are extremely volatile, so this is nothing like owning a Dividend King where you know what you’re getting with reasonable accuracy. BITO distributes based upon the formula in the link above, and the payout changes every single month. Over time, the distributions are meaningful to say the least, but the monthly payout range for the past year has been 36 cents to $1.77. Total distributions in the past 12 months is almost $12 per share, or more than half the current share price.

That means that if we’re going to track BITO’s progress against simply owning Bitcoin itself – which is a logical alternative – we need to look at total returns, not simply price returns.

For the past year, Bitcoin itself has outperformed BITO on a total return basis by about 15%. That’s a lot, and if you want to get the best exposure to Bitcoin, you’ll need a way to do that directly. However, if you like the ease and accessibility of owning it through an exchange-traded fund, BITO offers a nice alternative. The other thing is that BITO offers strong income possibilities, whereas Bitcoin obviously doesn’t, and never will. That income can be reinvested for more shares of BITO, or it can fund your lifestyle. It’s just a different way to gain exposure, and it’s not for everyone.

Where is BITO headed?

I have remained bullish on Bitcoin and linked funds for some time, and nothing about that has changed. The pattern in BITO looks very much to me like a bull flag, and if that’s right, BITO (and Bitcoin) are headed much higher.

BITO ran from the April low of $15 to a new high of $22.56, all the while paying its distributions. There was a 50-day SMA test in the past week, from which BITO bounced immediately and significantly. The flag pattern is filling out very nicely, the upper and lower bounds of which you can see above.

In addition, the PPO has made a centerline test and the histogram is looking like it’s about to go positive. If that occurs, it makes the breakout direction from the flag much more likely to be up.

Flags are consolidation patterns, so they are likely to resolve in the direction of prior trend. That was obviously an uptrend, and it amounted to about $7 per share from the April low. Should the flag resolve to the upside – which I fully expect – we should be looking at a similar move in BITO. That would be something like $29 as a target. We’ll see, but the risk/reward here is excellent as you can use the 50-day SMA, or the bottom of the flag pattern as a stop.

Wrapping up

If you are looking for direct exposure one-for-one with Bitcoin, BITO probably isn’t the best choice for that. The price doesn’t strictly track Bitcoin because BITO pays such massive distributions, and has a bit of tracking error from the futures it owns. On a total return basis, as well as the correlation of prices between the two, it is quite good. BITO offers income in a big way, but also the ease and convenience of owning Bitcoin exposure through an exchange-traded product rather than needing a wallet to hold Bitcoin.

I continue to like Bitcoin, and I continue to like BITO as an alternative way to gain exposure to Bitcoin upside. I’m retaining my strong buy on BITO here.

data.title: Procter & Gamble: A Juggernaut Cannot Escape Consumers Being Pressured
data.description:
At a time when many consumer packaged food companies are struggling in a major way, a company like Procter & Gamble (NYSE:PG) which of course, is not just any company, as it largely focuses on (personal) care and enjoys unparalleled scale advantage, is doing a bit better.

This relative outperformance is not necessarily inspiring, with organic sales not convincing for Procter as well. With topline sales keeping track of inflation at best, an earnings yield of just over 4% seems too aggressive given the performance and risk-free rate, as investors rely too much on an impressive dividend track record.

A Personal Care Juggernaut

Procter & Gamble is somewhat of a different animal because of its huge size and breath across personal care portfolio. The company is very well diversified and holds leading market share and own brands across various brands categories, with a line-up including the likes of Gillette, Braun, Head & Shoulders, Oral-B, Vicks, Tide, Pampers, and many more.

For the fiscal year of 2024 (which ended in June last year) the company reported a 2% increase in sales to $84 billion with revenues reported across 5 categories, other than a minor other category. The composition of sales was similar (or even a bit better) to other consumer packaged businesses with prices up 4%, volumes being flat and adverse currency moves shedding off 2% from growth.

The largest of these is a $29 billion fabric & home care business, followed by a $20 billion baby, feminine and healthcare business, a $15 billion beauty business and $11 billion healthcare business and a more than $6 billion grooming business. All these segments posted margins which come well in their twenties, with corporate costs being relatively modest at just over $2 billion.

Inflation has lifted the boat of Procter & Gamble as well, as 20% sales growth was seen between 2015 and 2025 (with revenues up from $70 billion to $844 million). However, this is a bit underwhelming given inflation observed in the meantime, although that P&G traditionally is light on M&A as well. That however tells just part of the story, as the company managed to grow operating income from levels around 20% of sales to levels in the mid-twenties, while the company bought back nearly 15% of its shares as well.

All this has driven a solid share price performance as well, as the triple whammy of sales growth, margin gains and reduction in the share base made that shares have risen from levels around the $80 mark in 2015, to $160 today. Shares have doubled, after shares hit a high of $180 in the past year.

Valuation Discussions

A $160 stock has commanded premium multiples on the back of this sold track record, with 2024 adjusted earnings, which are called ¨core earnings¨ in the case of Procter & Gamble came in at $6.59 per share, as reported last summer.

Net debt was relatively modest at $23.0 billion, very modest as based on GAAP accounting EBITDA approached $21 billion, for a leverage ratio around 1 times. The company called for organic growth into 2025, which should allow earnings to advance to $6.98 per share, despite a half a billion revenue headwind from currency moves, set to hurt earnings by twenty cents per share.

So far this year, results have been rather flattish. In April, Procter reported a 2% fall in third quarter sales, with organic sales up 1% for the period, comprised out of a 1% contribution from pricing, a 1% offset from lower volumes and complemented by continued currency headwinds (while the latter, of course, might turn soon given dollar weakness).

Net debt ticked up a bit to $25 billion, the result of buybacks and dividends. As a result of all this, full-year earnings are seen up to just $6.77 per share, amidst flattish sales, as Procter cannot escape the impact of consumers strapped for inflation as well.

This results in a 23 and change times earnings multiple, for an earnings yield of just over 4%, with many of these dividends paid out to investors. In April, the company announced a substantial 5% increase in the quarterly dividend, payable at $1.05 per share each quarter, with annual payouts exceeding $4.22 per share. This represents a payout ratio of nearly two-thirds of earnings with dividends paid each year since its founding in 1890, having been raised for 69 years in a row!

What Now?

The truth is that Procter has a solid track record, but most of this has come from margin gains and capital allocated to buybacks over the past year, with topline sales trends lagging if we adjust for inflation.

This is troublesome, as increasing these margins again from current levels is tough as operating margins in the mid-twenties are rich and fat, with consumers across the world strapped with their budgets, as is evident in recent organic growth trends.

With earnings growth close to non-existing this year, the company is feeling the pinch as well, as it announced plans to cut 7,000 jobs in the coming two years, equal to 15% of the non-manufacturing workforce, in an effort to streamline the organization and take care of inflationary pressures.

This could help, as a weaker dollar is a real near term tailwind as well, with 50% of the sales generated abroad, pushing up the in dollar reported results in all likelihood in the near term.

In either case, we are dealing with an under leveraged business, yet one which still commands a premium valuation, with consumer packaged companies fetching much lower multiples in the mid- to high-teens, a huge discount compared to Procter & Gamble. On the other hand, while Procter's current growth rate is underwhelming, it actually exceeds that of its food consumer packaged peers.

While investing is also a relative game, the absolute numbers do not make me very enthusiastic as shares have been holding up well, and a >20 times earnings multiple look too demanding to see appeal given the performance in more recent times, as the past (dividend) track record is not enough to provide appeal here.

While the company is still trading in a long-term range, it is a high-interest rate environment and consistent underperformance which makes me cautious here. I recognize that the aristocrat currently provides a dividend yield which provides support to the shares as well, yet it comes with higher payout ratios over time which increase the risk (if not for relatively modest leverage).

All this creates a tough set-up for sustainable outperformance here, making me generally cautious here, as organic growth in recent years simply has been too soft here.

data.title: Columbus Circle Capital I: Ugly Merger, Interesting Trade
data.description:
This week, SPAC (special purpose acquisition company) Columbus Circle Capital Corp. I (NASDAQ:CCCM) announced plans to merge with ProCap BTC LLC. As a SPAC, CCC is just a pile of cash; ProCap is essentially just a pile of Bitcoin. At the current CCCM stock price of $10.73, and the current BTC price of just over $107,000, the merger values each dollar in ProCap's Bitcoin stash at about $1.43.

Whether that's a good deal is a matter of perspective. One view is that paying $1.43 in cash for $1 in Bitcoin is ludicrous on its face. Investors can buy Bitcoin for pretty close to $1, whether via direct ownership or an exchange-traded fund. ProCap's structure does suggest some leverage to the BTC price, but that leverage is not guaranteed, and investors have myriad ways to gain levered exposure to Bitcoin, all of which appear to be lower-cost.

The contrary view, however, is that 1.43:1 is actually a pretty good ratio for what have become known as Bitcoin treasury companies. The pioneer and the largest of the group obviously is Strategy (MSTR), which has an enterprise value 1.87x the value of its Bitcoin holdings. There's some value in Strategy's legacy software business, but at this point not much; even accounting for those operations, the premium here is in the range of 80%. Other companies (130, per the CCC/ProCap merger presentation) have executed similar strategies in Bitcoin and other coins and gotten bigger premiums; Metaplanet (OTC:MTPLF) is valued at nearly 5x its Bitcoin stash.

The problem for ProCap, however, is that the initial response to the deal itself suggests the post-merger entity is not likely to follow the MSTR path, let alone that of Metaplanet. Meanwhile, ProCap chief executive officer Anthony Pompliano, a longtime investor (and Twitter/X personality and podcast host), has made some comments about creating an operating business here as well, but it's not clear exactly that is supposed to be. To top it off, the structure of the capital raise beyond the SPAC cash suggests a real possibility of a wave of pre-merger redemptions.

All told, it's hard to have too much optimism about the post-merger entity here. But with CCCM stock not that far above the redemption price, there is an interesting pre-merger trade to be had, with a low-risk option on a spike in BTC and/or a return to a more optimistic outlook toward crypto treasury companies more broadly.

The Deal Structure For CCCM Stock

The post-merger ProCap will have $1 billion in capital, raised from three sources:

The SPAC has $256 million in cash in trust, thanks to a $250 million initial public offering last month. The $517 million in equity already has been raised, with most of the funds used to buy BTC over the last two days. The convertibles will be issued at the merger close.

On its face, this doesn't seem like a bad deal: ProCap is raising $1 billion and buying just shy of 9,500 BTC (note that the presentation was based on a BTC price of $100,000). But a closer look shows that SPAC investors are being disadvantaged here. It also explains why the initial optimism toward the deal, spiked by leaked details to the Financial Times, has faded so dramatically:

Notably, the preferred equity is getting into the SPAC at $8 per share (each preferred stock unit converts to common stock at a 1.25x ratio). The notes convert at $13 per share, but they're also 2x collateralized by Bitcoin (or cash, but as seen above the post-merger ProCap will have minimal cash).

Paying $10.70 For $7.50 (Or Less)

Because of both the discount offered the preferred equity holder and a pretty substantial promote for Pompliano, CCCM investors wind up getting what looks like less than their fair share:

In the indicative example, with the BTC price at $100,000, post-merger ProCap would be have a net asset value of $955 million ($950 million in BTC plus $5 million in cash). SPAC shareholders would get 19.7%, or $188 million; even at $10, the premium is about 1.33x.

In reality, the numbers are a bit different. ProCap already has purchased 4,932 BTC this week for a total of $514 million. That leaves $436 million in cash (from the SPAC and the converts), for an NAV of $950 million, or a per-share NAV of $7.50. The current price of $10.70 is thus about a 1.43x premium.

But even that is a bit inflated, because it assumes the convertibles actually convert into stock. Treat the converts as debt (as would be the case with CCCM and/or the post-merger stock below $13) and NAV is actually $715 million across 108.6 million shares. That's a per-share figure of just $6.58, suggesting a current premium of about 63%. (It's also an example of just how important the convertibles actually converting is to the success of the treasury plan.)

Two Post-Merger Problems For Columbus Circle Capital I

The structure here creates two problems assuming the merger goes through. The first is looking to the downside and upside. Even if the cash from the SPAC and the converts buys BTC at $100K (adding 4,910 BTC, essentially doubling the current stash), a move in the Bitcoin price to $80K would leave net asset value somewhere around $550 million (again, treating the converts as debt, so 108.6 million shares outstanding and $230M in net debt). Net asset value here is a little over $5; in this scenario, it's not hard to imagine CCCM dropping below $7.

In other words, the downside here is amplified even with some kind of treasury premium: a ~25% decline in Bitcoin leads to a 35%-plus drop in CCCM stock. But the converse is not necessarily true.

The SPAC agreement includes "adjustment shares", which will be issued to the sponsor and the preferred equity investors if BTC rises between now and the close. Basically, their number of shares increases by the same percentage BTC does. Imagine, for instance, BTC doubling; the share of equity owned by SPAC shareholders in the post-merger ProCap drops by half. NAV ($7.50 per share assuming conversion) only goes to about $10; at the same premium, CCCM stock would be worth less than $15.

For this to work, then, the premium has to expand. In theory, post-merger this can happen (and indeed it has happened for MSTR, which has consistently outpaced Bitcoin). But part of the reason Strategy has outperformed is that it keeps adding to its Bitcoin haul on the way up. It would seem that ProCap has designs on a similar strategy (no pun intended). The issue is that the post-merger structure means that the company will have no cash to adjust its holdings.

Pompliano and ProCap claim to have a workaround, however, as witnessed by the last two bullet points on this slide:

The problem is that the strategy here, such as it is, is basically to buy (Bitcoin) low and sell (CCCM stock) high. In bullet point #2, "Strategically rais[ing] capital during favorable market cycles to scale our Bitcoin holdings" means that ProCap will issue equity and/or converts when the demand is there. "Tactical accumulation, including strategic dips, volatility harvesting, and programmatic purchases aligned with macro signals and capital cycles" is a long way of saying that ProCap wants to buy the coin low.

Buying low and selling high perhaps can work for an expert trader. For a publicly traded company, however, it's a much more difficult endeavor. Is ProCap going to have an at-the-market offering that just dumps shares every time the premium to BTC expands? That seems like an instantly recognizable and consistent overhang. Is it in turn going to buy every dip in BTC? That's certainly been a smart strategy for the past 15-plus years, but, again, ProCap has to actually raise the cash to make those buys. More broadly, there's no real evidence that Pompliano (or his associates) have the acumen to move into Bitcoin and out of the stock in a way that is accretive to shareholder value rather than investment bank and exchange fees.

To be fair, Pompliano has said that ProCap will build a business on top of the Bitcoin stash. As he told CNBC in an interview:

I believe that the future of these companies is going to be how do you build revenue and profit with financial services on top of that. No different than if you look at the traditional financial service firms. They have a balance sheet of dollars, and they go and they build these financial services on top that drive revenue and profit. I think that you still have to build a business on top of a big pile of Bitcoin.

But there's been zero discussion of what these "financial services" are supposed to be. Pompliano told CNBC that he and ProCap "sit at the intersection between the Internet and the Bitcoiners and the institutional world." That's been a good business for Pompliano personally — he himself is investing $8.5 million in the deal — but there is no real evidence that positioning will create a good business for ProCap shareholders.

Owning CCCM And Not ProCap

And yet, with the pullback below $11, there is an intriguing potential trade here. The merger presentation cited $256 million in trust value for the CCCM SPAC, meaning redemption value should be $10.24 per share. That suggests 4%-5% downside from the current level (the cash in trust will increase over time as interest is accrued, but investors still will be down 4%-5% relative to simply holding a money market fund or short-term Treasuries).

That's not a terrible downside given that there is some potential upside between now and when the redemption deadline for the merger arrives. A sharp rally in BTC would probably bleed over here. Meanwhile, the merger presentation points out Pompliano's impressive online reach. At least from a Twitter/X search, Bitcoin fans seem mostly disappointed with the deal, (trading in CCCM obviously suggests the same) but Pompliano has the time and the megaphone to change sentiment.

Indeed, we've already seen a similar trade work in another SPAC, Colombier Acquisition Corp. II (CLBR). CLBR too saw an early retail-driven rally (this one being more political; Donald Trump Jr. is involved with its target, GrabAGun) that faded, only to bounce back strongly. When I covered the name in February, CLBR was barely above its redemption price; it's since gained 35%.

CLBR was probably a better trade than CCCM; the structure here (particularly as the adjustment shares are understood) seems like it's leading toward a quite high redemption rate. Given that the premium to BTC for a treasury company is based on sentiment, the early response to the merger announcement itself is a red flag longer-term.

But with 4-5% downside, and the volatility inherent in anything remotely exposed to crypto, CCCM seems like an interesting trade, too — at least until shares no longer can be redeemed. After that, the outlook looks much murkier, and much worse.

data.title: Why Smart Investors Are Watching GitLab Closely Right Now
data.description:
GitLab (NASDAQ:GTLB) is a U.S.-based company with international operations that offers an end-to-end software development platform. Revenues from U.S. accounts 81%, 16% from Europe and 2% from Asia Pacific. According to the company, its DevSecOps platform differentiates from its main competitor platform, Microsoft’s GitHub, due to provide all the necessary tools for every stage of the software development lifecycle -from the initial planning of an idea to the final delivery of the product to the customer.

For its clients this means that they need only GTLB’s platform without the need for additional third-party tools to develop and manage software products. As a result, GitLab helps reduce costs for its customers.

The company’s price performance has declined by approximately 25% ytd, falling from a high of $73.14 to current price of around $42.60. Investors need to assess whether this drop has fundamental or other major reason that justify this decline or not. But first let’s to a deep dive into company operations.

Does GitLab Have a Competitive Advantage?

Well, I may not be an expert in how Software as a Service (SaaS) products are built, but as a professional who uses these platforms daily, I have confidence in my opinion: GitLab has a competitive advantage. Let me explain!

According to a Forrester Consulting research, GitLab’s DevSecOps platform, reduce costs for company’s clients, as already mentioned. As a result, clients can realize up to a 483% return on investment within three years. Moreover, GitLab customers see up to 15x faster time to first release, enabling them to meet the growing business demand to deliver new capabilities and increase responsiveness to change.

It's ‘shift left’ security strategy -which means critical steps like testing and vulnerability detection are executed earlier in the development lifecycle- eliminate the need for multiple security tools. An aspect which aligns with the automated audit and compliance standards that organization request to follow. As a result, customers achieve approximately $5.9 million in cost savings through improved security efficiency.

Lastly and top of the above, GitLab’s AI-powered suite, GitLab Duo, integrates AI across every stage of the development process, enhancing both productivity and software quality.

Financial and My Long-Term View

GitLab has experienced impressive revenue growth from FY19 to FY24, primarily driven by its Subscription-self-managed and SaaS segment. This segment includes organizations that either use GTLB’s cloud servers or deploy the platform on their own infrastructure through an Enterprise Edition license. The Subscription segment accounts about 89% of GitLab’s total revenue, while License-self-managed and other segment, contributes the remaining portion. The License segment sell perpetual licenses for on-premises use of GitLab’s platform - this model typically targets large organizations such as Banks.

The Subscription segment is the most important component of company’s business model and shows an average annual revenue growth of 59% from FY19 to FY20. While the License segment grew at a 54%, over the same period. Even better is that company’s debt has maintained at very low levels, about $392 million as of FY24, supporting financial stability and flexibility for future growth.

Although company’s impressive revenue growth, it remains unprofitable. As a fast-growing company, GitLab requires significant investment in operating expenses such as selling and marketing expenses in order to attract new customers and expand its customer base. So, I see reasonable that in FY24, 51% of total revenue was allocated to marketing expenses, while 32% went to R&D and 25% to G&A.

A positive signal of company’s solid business model is the downward trend in its OPEX, as the above left table indicates. For comparison, in FY19, marketing expenses represented 122% of total revenue, R&D 73% and G&A 51%. This means that by FY24, the company has reduced its OPEX as a percentage of revenue by approximately half.

In my view, I see OPEX to rise in absolute terms alongside revenue growth, as the company is focused on building a strong customer base and gaining market share. However, I would closely monitor its OPEX trend. Therefore, the company appears to be in an early-stage growth phase, spending a significant amount on OPEX and I believe it can expand further due to its efficient business model and competitive advantage. Nevertheless, its proven revenue growth indicates that these expenditures are effective in generating growth, and profitability is likely a matter of time.

Performance and Momentum

Companies that offer SaaS services have specific metrics to indicate their performance, the Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR) and the Net Revenue Retention (NRR).

ARR measure the company’s ability to acquire new customers and expand its services to existing ones. If ARR is rising, it suggests that the company is growing. NRR is a key metric for evaluating company’s ability both to retain and grow revenue from its existing customer base. An NRR greater than 100% indicates that the company grow revenue from its current customer.

As the table below illustrates, both ARR and NRR remained strong from FY19 to FY20, with NRR consistently above 100% during this period. Additionally, ARR from significant customers which generate more than $1 million continued to grow, demonstrating GTLB’s ability to provide solutions to large organizations. Furthermore, the company increased its customer base by approximately 300% over the same period, from 2,475 in FY20 to 9,893 customers in FY24.

Company has already shown impressive revenue growth, but investors need to assess whether the company has more room to grow. The answer exists in company’s quarterly reports. ARR and Customer base have shown yoy growth of 26% and 13%, respectively, while NRR declined by 5% but remains above 100%. A positive signal for GTLB’s sustainability.

All key performance metrics indicate an already strong track record and positive momentum. Given this, the application development software industry, in which GTLB operates, is expected to grow at a GAGR of 23.9% from 2024 to 2032, with North America holding a market share of 33.72% in 2024.

As a takeaway, I believe the company has very strong financial with impressive revenue growth. The momentum and industry prospects are more than solid; however, investors should continue monitoring the company’s profitability and OPEX trend.

Valuation and Risks

Consider the company’s negative net income and its large-scale main competitor, MSFT, is better to focus on specific key valuations metrics.

Both the EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios indicate a significantly overvalued company, as shown in the table below. A substantial portion of this overvaluation is justified by the company’s expected revenue growth both yoy and fwd. Specifically, company’s fwd revenue growth is projected to reach up to 25.6%, compared to the sector’s 7.1%, which is an impressive difference.

Investors need to assess whether the current valuation ratios truly reflect a fast-growth company like GTLB. In my view, the company has huge growth potential, and its current share price may already incorporate part of this growth. However, given that the company is still in early stage of scaling its operations, I have less trust on these traditional valuation metrics considering a long-term investment.

Given that the company, expects revenue to range between $936 - $942 million in FY26, -representing approximately 24% yoy revenue growth, compared to $760 in FY25- I strongly believe that GTLB has more room to grow and the current valuation metrics don’t fully reflect the company’s long-term growth potential.

Risk Analysis

As already mentioned, GTLB operates in a highly competitive industry and its main competitor is MSFT, among others. What that means? This presents a significant challenge.

MSFT operates at a totally different scale, generating high profits and holding a strong cash position. Therefore, it has the ability to invest heavily in R&D and marketing in order to develop a platform with comparable capabilities with GitLab’s platform. If that happens, GTLB will lose its completive advantage.

Company’s industry is also highly sensitive to data breaches. A potential breach of company clients’ data could severely damage the company’s reputation and, in the end its revenue growth and overall performance.

The company is not yet well-established as its competitors; therefore, it remains sensitive to competition and potential security breaches, which could immediately negative affect its profit margins through price pressures and its expansion. Although the company has demonstrated an impressive track record investors need to assess its long-term sustainability before investing, as in my view, this is a high-risk, high-reward opportunity.

Conclusion

In conclusion, GitLab is an undervalue stock with attractive growth potential, strong financials and positive momentum. While GTLB operates in a highly competitive industry with well-established peers, and for this reason is a high-risk, high-reward opportunity. I believe the company has significant room to grow, as it is still in early-stage growth phase. Given that also still holds a competitive advantage by offering an end-to-end solution, thought its platform, I rate company as a BUY.

data.title: Five Strong High-Yield Corporate Bond ETFs
data.description:
With credit spreads widening, thought to have a quick look at five strong high-yield corporate bond ETFs. These are:

The iShares Broad USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (USHY), the largest high-yield bond ETF in the market. With an incredibly well-diversified portfolio and a 6.7% yield, USHY is a good, vanilla, choice, without any significant advantages or disadvantages to peers.

The Schwab High Yield Bond ETF (SCYB), the cheapest high-yield bond ETF in the market, with a 0.03% expense ratio, 7.0% dividend yield. Lower expense ratios necessarily increase (reduce by less) shareholder returns, which should result in SCYB marginally outperforming most of its peers.

The iShares High Yield Systematic Bond ETF (HYDB), something of a smart beta fund, focusing on bonds with comparatively high yields for their level of risk, and with a 7.0% dividend yield. Stronger performance track-record than its peers, but also shorter.

The VanEck Fallen Angel High Yield Bond ETF (ANGL) and the iShares Fallen Angels USD Bond ETF (FALN), both of which invest in corporate bonds downgraded from investment-grade to non-investment grade. These bonds tend to experience forced selling, trading with comparatively low prices, which means sizable gains as prices recover. Both ETFs have outperformed since inception, but their 6.2% yields are a bit lower than average.

High-Yield Corporate Bond ETFs - Quick Overview and Investment Thesis

All high-yield corporate bond ETFs focus on, well, high-yield corporate bonds, securities with a specific set of features, which imply a common set of characteristics for these ETFs. Let's have a quick look at some of these.

High-yield corporate bond ETFs offer comparatively higher yields, with these five ETFs yielding almost twice as much as the Vanguard Total Bond Market Index Fund ETF (BND), the largest bond ETF in the market, and industry benchmark.

Higher yields result in higher long-term returns, although there might be significant short-term volatility during downturns and recessions. Returns since inception are as expected, SCYB excluded from below as the fund is much younger than its peers:

High-yield corporate bond ETFs focus on comparatively riskier bonds, with significant investments in bonds rated BB, sizable investments in those rated B. Credit risk is high but not excessive. Expect moderate losses during downturns and recessions, higher losses when compared to investment-grade bonds. Returns during early 2020, the onset of the coronavirus pandemic, are representative of this.

High-yield corporate bonds are generally short-term bonds because investors are reticent to extend long-term credit to riskier borrowers, as it's simply too risky. Duration is generally below-average, so expect less sensitivity to rates, lower losses when rates increase, and lower gains when these decrease. Returns since early 2022, when the Fed started to hike rates, are a good example of this.

The high-yield corporate bond ETFs above all have somewhat similar dividend yields, performance track-records, and risk. Although these are broadly similar investments, there are some important differences between them. Let's have a look.

USHY - Simple High-Yield Index ETF

USHY is the largest high-yield bond ETF in the market, with almost $23B in AUM. It is a simple high-yield index ETF, tracking a broad-based index of these securities. It is an incredibly vanilla choice, without any significant advantages or disadvantages to peers. Its size and diversification are minor advantages though, the first increasing liquidity, the second somewhat reducing risk, volatility, and potential losses from an individual defaults. It has a 6.7% dividend yield, 0.08% expense ratio, and a somewhat below-average performance track-record, dependent on the specific time period analyzed.

I last covered USHY here.

SCYB - Cheapest High-Yield Bond ETF

SCYB is the cheapest high-yield bond ETF in the market, at least that I am aware of, with a 0.03% expense ratio. It is also a simple high-yield index ETF, with comparable characteristics to USHY. SCYB's cheapness is its key differentiator, and key advantage relative to peers. It is also a relatively small advantage, with the fund being only 0.05% cheaper than USHY. That amounts to $50 annual savings for a $100,000 portfolio, which isn't nothing, but definitely not a whole lot. Normal volatility, turnover, tracking error, and small differences in indexes could easily outweigh the difference in expenses. SCYB has slightly underperformed USHY since inception, so these factors have outweighed the expenses since inception:

Notwithstanding that last point, SCYB remains a reasonable enough high-yield bond ETF, with a diversified portfolio and a 7.0% dividend yield. It could outperform moving forward and, at the very least, underperformance is far from guaranteed.

I last covered SCYB here.

HYDB - Systematic High-Yield Bond ETF

HYDB is a high-yield bond ETF focusing on bonds with comparatively higher yields for their credit quality. Something akin to investing in the highest-yielding BB-rated bonds, then the highest-yielding B-rated bonds, and so on. HYDB's strategy should result in higher dividend yields than peers, as is (mostly) the case:

HYDB's strategy aims for similar risk and drawdowns to peers, with the fund seeing similar losses to USHY during the pandemic, higher than ANGL and FALN. Results compared to the latter two were due to characteristics of these ETFs, and not due to HYDB itself being riskier than expected, but more on this later.

HYDB's strategy aims for some measure of outperformance, with slight outperformance being likelier than significant (the strategy isn't too aggressive). The fund has slightly outperformed since inception, consistent with an effective strategy.

HYDB sports a 0.35% expense ratio, slightly higher than USHY and SCYB, but far from excessive.

Overall, it seems that HYDB is the slightly more expensive choice, but with higher return potential. It seems like a reasonable choice, but do remember that the higher return potential is just that, and that the fund might not continue to outperform moving forward.

I last covered HYDB here.

ANGL and FALN - Fallen Angel ETFs

Both ANGL and FALN focus on fallen angels, or corporate bonds recently downgraded from investment-grade to non-investment-grade.

Fallen angels tend to experience forced selling, as some institutional investors are prevented from investing or holding riskier non-investment grade bonds and so must sell any holding of theirs that becomes a fallen angel. As an example, the iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD) only invests in investment-grade bonds, so if any of its holdings is downgraded to high-yield, LQD would be forced into selling said holding. With almost $31B in AUM, said sale would almost certainly have an impact on the market.

Due to the above, fallen angels tend to start with comparatively low prices / high yields. Conditions improve as markets settle, leading to higher prices in time, and long-term outperformance. In theory, at least. In practice, fallen angels have outperformed high-yield bonds for decades:

As have both ANGL and FALN. Only including ANGL below, as that is the older ETF, and benchmarking it to a longer-running high-yield corporate bond index ETF.

On a more negative note, recent returns have been decisively weaker, as the above-average duration of these ETFs was a significant headwind from 2022 to 2024, as the Fed hiked rates. Duration is above-average, as these bonds were originally investment-grade, with tend to have higher durations than high-yield bonds. I'm seeing similar returns to the benchmark these past five years, which isn't bad, but definitely worse than before.

As these bonds were originally investment-grade, their coupon rates are lower, as are the dividend yields of these ETFs.

In my opinion, the stronger performance track-records of these ETFs more than outweigh their comparatively low yields. Some income investors might disagree.

I last covered ANGL here, FALN here.

Conclusion

USHY, SCYB, HYDB, ANGL, and FALN are all strong high-yield corporate bond ETFs, and buys.

USHY is the broadest, simplest ETF, and a balanced, vanilla choice.

SCYB is similar to USHY, but with a marginally lower expense ratio.

HYDB targets a smaller section of the high-yield corporate bond market, aiming to outperform without increasing risk.

ANGL and FALN focus on fallen angels, high-yield corporate bonds with significant long-term outperformance, average medium-term returns, below-average yields.

Of the above, I slightly prefer ANGL and FALN to the rest, but the other choices are still good, and some income investors might prefer higher-yielding alternatives to these two funds.

data.title: D-Wave Faces A Classical Reality Check
data.description:
Intro

Retail investors are pouring into quantum-computing equities, but the shine can quickly fade once the numbers hit the tape. D-Wave Quantum (NYSE:QBTS) has enjoyed a spectacular share-price run in 2025, briefly eclipsing a $5 billion market capitalization despite booking just $15 million of revenue in the March quarter and a cumulative deficit in excess of $630 million. The company’s pitch centers on quantum annealing, a technology that management says already delivers “quantum advantage” on real-world optimization problems. Yet the wider industry is racing toward universal, gate-based systems able to support large-scale error correction and a vastly broader algorithmic toolkit. Investors must decide whether D-Wave’s specialized architecture is an enduring niche or an evolutionary dead end.

Thesis

Gate-based quantum platforms from IBM (IBM), Google (GOOG) and IonQ (IONQ) are scaling qubit counts and lowering error rates at a pace that is compressing the available headroom for annealing-only systems. IBM’s publicly detailed roadmap targets fault-tolerant machines with thousands of logical qubits by 2029, while Google’s latest Willow chip advances the company’s own million-qubit ambitions later in the decade. IonQ, a pure-play rival, has laid out a modular architecture it says can photonic-ally network many cores to reach the same scale. These platforms can, in principle, execute algorithm families such as Shor’s and Grover’s that annealers cannot, opening far larger addressable markets in cryptography, quantum chemistry and machine learning.

D-Wave remains committed to annealing and cites recent peer-reviewed work in Science as proof that its Advantage machines outperform the best classical supercomputers on certain magnetic-material simulations. Critics counter that those demonstrations involve highly structured cost-functions tailor-made for annealers and do not generalize to universal algorithms. If the gate-based cohort crosses the million-qubit threshold with effective error correction, annealing risks being crowded out of research budgets and customer mindshare, relegating D-Wave to a boutique optimization tool rather than a broadly useful platform.

Competitive Landscape: Gate-Based Momentum

IBM’s Quantum System Two installation in Japan underscores how quickly gate hardware is moving from the lab to production environments. The Heron processor driving that system delivers lower two-qubit error rates and deeper circuit depth, attributes that translate directly into algorithmic reach. Google’s Willow chip announcement likewise focused on sustained logical fidelity across hundreds of cycles, a prerequisite for scaling to millions of physical qubits. Importantly, both companies enjoy R&D budgets measured in billions and benefit from cloud franchises that can subsidize quantum investments.

IonQ and Rigetti, though smaller, have adopted trapped-ion and superconducting gate modalities respectively and continue to raise capital at valuations well above fundamental revenue. IonQ’s market cap hovers around $10.7 billion, and its stock still trades near 200× forward sales. That access to capital allows aggressive hiring of quantum-information scientists who might otherwise have gravitated to annealing research. The funnel of talent and funding toward gate-based work reduces the likelihood that annealing will set the pace for the industry’s next leap.

Commercial Adoption Stalls

Despite management’s celebration of “record” quarterly results, D-Wave’s customer metrics reveal limited traction. The company counted 133 total customers over the trailing four quarters, up only five from the prior period, and bookings collapsed 64% year over year to $1.6 million. The standout $15 million revenue print was driven by a single hardware sale to the Jülich Supercomputing Centre, a lumpy transaction unlikely to recur every quarter. Absent hardware, the subscription and professional-services business remains small and grossly insufficient to support rising operating costs.

By contrast, IonQ guided 2025 revenue of $75–$95 million, reflecting multiyear contracts with both private-sector and government accounts. IBM and Google package quantum access within their existing cloud offerings, making consumption frictionless for enterprise developers. D-Wave’s pay-per-use Leap platform lacks comparable distribution and faces the uncomfortable truth that many optimization problems can still be addressed faster and cheaper with classical heuristic solvers running on GPUs.

Capital Structure and Shareholder Dilution

D-Wave ended March with $304 million in cash after tapping its third at-the-market program for $146 million in net proceeds during the quarter. Management touts this war chest as proof of a “self-funding” path to profitability, but the flipside is relentless dilution. Shares outstanding ballooned to 291 million from 161 million a year earlier, a 81% increase. Less than two weeks ago the company filed for an additional $400 million shelf registration, signaling that equity issuance will remain a primary liquidity lever. Past ATM programs of $150–$175 million were completed in mere days, suggesting strong trading appetite but also highlighting the gap between operating cash burn and organic inflows.

Warrant liabilities of $66 million and a residual $38 million equity line of credit further complicate the cap table. Any meaningful recovery in the share price incentivizes warrant exercise, adding incremental dilution precisely when public investors might hope for upside optionality. With adjusted EBITDA still negative $6.1 million on just $15 million of sales, the capital market remains the de-facto lifeline.

Financials

Revenue surged 509% year over year to $15 million, lifting GAAP gross margin to a lofty 92.5% thanks to the high-margin hardware deal. Excluding that sale, management admitted on the call that run-rate subscription revenue “remains in the low single-digit millions,” implying far lower sustainable margin. Operating expenses rose 31% to $25.2 million, driven chiefly by head-count growth and marketing spend. The net result was a $5.4 million loss and negative operating cash flow of $19.3 million.

The balance sheet shows $325.6 million of total assets against $118.2 million of liabilities, but equity is inflated by $850 million of additional paid-in capital rather than retained earnings. Deferred revenue fell sharply to $6.2 million across current and non-current buckets, reflecting the recognition of the Jülich system sale and thin new bookings. Without another headline hardware contract, revenue may revert to the $2–$4 million quarterly range seen through 2024, resurrecting high cash-burn dynamics.

Management offered no explicit 2025 top-line guidance, instead reiterating nebulous targets for “25%–35% compound growth” once the Advantage2 system is widely available. Investors should view that ambition against consensus expectations for IonQ to reach $138 million of revenue by 2027 and for IBM to integrate quantum into hybrid HPC workflows that could unlock billions in incremental cloud spend.

Risks

The principal risk to a bearish stance is that gate-based progress stalls. Quantum error correction remains a formidable hurdle, and industry timelines have slipped before. Should IBM, Google or IonQ miss their qubit-scaling milestones, D-Wave might extend its first-mover status in commercial quantum computing longer than skeptics expect. Another mitigant is a possible surge in optimization demand from logistics and AI inferencing workloads that map naturally onto annealers.

Conversely, liquidity concerns could crystallize faster than modeled. If equity markets sour on speculative tech, D-Wave’s ability to monetize its ATM shelf may dry up, forcing management to consider cost cuts or strategic alternatives at unfavorable valuations. Regulatory scrutiny is an emerging wildcard; the U.S. Department of Energy recently announced preliminary export-control reviews for quantum hardware that interfaces with high-performance classical systems, a category that could include D-Wave’s next-generation Advantage2 installations. Any licensing friction would delay revenue recognition and exacerbate cash burn.

Conclusion

D-Wave trades on a Price/Sales multiple of roughly 147× trailing twelve-month revenue, well above IonQ at about 192× and Rigetti at 268× but all vastly richer than the S&P 500’s 3× average. With negative earnings, P/E and PEG are not meaningful, and enterprise value remains a function of cash on hand rather than sustainable EBITDA. Using adjusted EBITDA of negative $22 million annualized and enterprise value of $4.1 billion, EV/EBITDA is effectively non-computable. By comparison, IonQ’s EV/Sales near 230× and Rigetti’s 210–260× underscore that the sector as a whole prices in heroic growth assumptions, but at least those peers are aligned with the technology trajectory attracting most research dollars.

Absent clear evidence that optimization annealing can scale beyond niche workloads, the stock looks overvalued. If gate-based leaders achieve fault-tolerant million-qubit machines within the next five years, D-Wave risks being stranded with a single-purpose architecture commanding little enterprise premium. The current valuation affords little margin for error and assumes capital markets will remain receptive to continuous dilution. In my view the risk-reward skew is asymmetric to the downside, and I expect QBTS to underperform both quantum peers and the broader tech indices over the next 12-24 months.

data.title: VOO Vs. QQQ: Which Is The Better ETF For Investors In Their 30s
data.description:
Whether to go down the path of stock picking or ETF investing, particularly for younger investors (say in their 30s/40s) who have decades ahead to benefit from compounding is without doubt a timeless debate that I've discussed on multiple occasions in my articles and comment section.

Even as a stock picker myself, seeking performance alpha, there have been multiple times I have made the argument that the general investing community would be better off by simply parking their cash either in market-weighted ETFs or sector-specific ones, even as it may feel "too vanilla". Sometimes plain and simple is the way to go, even if there isn't thrill.

Contrarily, if you are picking stocks, particularly with the objective of delivering alpha over the broader market indices, you are nonstop tasked with due diligence to get ahead of investing trends (e.g. invest in Nvidia (NVDA) prior to everyone else doing so), reallocating capital and rebalancing it to balance risk/reward, incurring trade fees and risking inefficient capital allocation, or buying overpriced equities particularly as you add cash on a monthly basis from your salary to your investment account.

Yes, it may seem like I would be discouraging the younger investors from stock picking, and maybe partially I am, or at least encouraging to invest with a more balanced approach, e.g. 50% ETFs, 50% individual stocks. But at the same time, I am trying to make the argument that if your investment horizon is 20–30 years, outperforming broader market indices becomes an uphill battle with the odds stacked against you and without the need of my saying that 90% of professional investors lose the battle. What are the chances an individual investor outperforms? That's where ETFs like Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:VOO) and Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (NASDAQ:QQQ) become the ideal candidates as core portfolio holdings.

VOO's Diversification Vs. QQQ's Concentration

Let's take it step-by-step, with me kicking this off by listing the vital differences between the two ETFs, that seasoned investors may want to skip.

VOO tracks the S&P 500 index, which is comprised of the 500 largest US companies across all sectors. No matter if we speak about tech, financials, healthcare, REITs, energy or others, you'll find all of these sectors and stocks inside of this index, which essentially tracks the broader US economy and is market weighted. E.g. larger the company = higher percentage allocation. No doubt, VOO's diversification is far better than QQQs.

QQQ, on the other hand, tracks the Nasdaq-100 index, that is made up of the 100 largest non-financial companies listed on the Nasdaq exchange. The decision to exclude banks was taken back in 1985 (banks back then were among the largest businesses) and isn't a direct response to investor's falling appetite for banks after the Great Financial Crisis. As a consequence of it's more "picky" structure, QQQ is more tech and growth exposed, concentrated in richly valued stocks, with exposure to the latest trends, such as AI, cloud and other innovations. Rich valuation and sector concentration also leads to significantly higher volatility/risk, but returns as well.

Do You Want To Own 500 Stocks Or 100?

As you can see from the sector allocation in the table below, even though the outperformance of the tech sector has pushed VOO's exposure in this sector to over 30%+, risking overconcentration, VOO's overall sector exposure is more balanced compared to QQQs, yet not ideal if you ask me.

Inside of VOO, we'll find value-oriented names like Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B), JPMorgan (JPM) or Exxon (XOM), businesses that provide a layer of diversification away from tech-heavy exposure, reduce the overall valuation premium and might act defensively during overall market drawdowns with less cyclicality.

Even though, both of the ETFs are market-weighted, meaning mega-cap exposure is prevailing over the smaller businesses, QQQ's concentration within 100 stocks, compared to VOO's 500 does make a difference, particularly to offset tech-specific drawdown, which is synonymous with reduced CAPEX spending during economic downturns.

Also, while we see a small percentage of QQQ being exposed to areas like Healthcare, Industrials, Energy and Real Estate, the exposure simply isn't enough to overshadow tech's 57.2% weight.

As I like to call it, QQQ's exposure is oriented towards momentum sectors. This is great during tech bull markets — like the AI CAPEX super cycle — but, brutal when spending slows. I'll show you later.

Here we clearly see QQQ's concentration in its top 10 holdings (if we also account for NFLX, not shown below) is over 50% and significantly higher compared to VOO's 36%.

In practice, this means, that if you go 100% QQQ in your portfolio, you are exposing yourself predominantly only to mega-cap companies, with high conviction on growth. I wouldn't recommend this, as bear markets will be painful.
