Instead, my thinking is that, if you are 30–40 years old, with a long-time horizon, exposing your portfolio to high conviction, concentrated mega-caps with pristine balance sheets and industry-leading profitability, makes a lot of sense, but one needs to understand the concentration risk and ideally diversify in other areas of your portfolio.

QQQ's Performance Crushed VOO's, But So did Volatility

Concentration risk works both ways.

If we go back to January 2016 and invest $10,000 into a portfolio that's 100% VOO and another $10,000 into a second one with 100% exposure to QQQ, we earn very attractive returns both ways.

However, over this 10-year duration, including dividend reinvestment, QQQ's CAGR is 18.59% and VOO's is 13.88%. That's close to 5% outperformance for QQQ, which over longer durations, e.g. 20–30 years, would cause a massive difference in a level of wealth.

No matter if we reduce the time horizon, or increase it, we would get the same result. QQQ outperformed VOO, thanks to its concentration in the handful of companies that shaped the world in the last 2–3 decades (read smartphones, internet, semiconductors).

But it isn't just about returns.

We need to be mindful of the risks here as well. In the last 10 years, QQQ's worst drawdown was -33% back in 2022, during the recessionary fear that never materialized, whereas VOO's more diversified portfolio across sectors, mega/mid/small-caps helped it to withstand the shocks slightly better, falling "only" 18%. If the 2022-2023 recession materialized? We could have seen a +50% drawdown. Ouch!

This is clear evidence that while QQQ's higher volatility and concentration leads to more attractive returns, it comes with a deeper corrections and crashes. Yet, QQQ's Sharpe ratio of 0.75 — the measure of risk-adjusted returns I like to use — compared to VOO's 0.65, simply shows, that QQQ's risk-adjusted returns are simply superior.

But, could one stomach QQQ's volatility?

That's a question you'll have to answer for yourself, but QQQ does have the potential to work for portfolios as long as the investor exhibits a high level of emotional resilience and has 20–30 years time horizon to ride the swings and moods of the market. I would say buying QQQ without at least 10 years of horizon isn't a wise move.

QQQ's 10-20 year outperformance went hand-in-hand with tech's outperformance led by innovation from companies like Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta Platforms (META) to name a few, longer periods of low-interest rates that make future cashflows more valuable and recently the AI hype, that boosted cyclical semis, like Nvidia and Broadcom (AVGO).

But, make no mistake. Winners of today, like Nvidia, also in the past witnessed brutal volatility. Back in 2022, Nvidia's stock fell more than 50%, with investors, but insiders as well, selling shares en masse as interest rates spiked and the broader consumer electronics market entered a cyclical downturn. At some point, we'll likely see volatility like that again.

If insiders couldn't see the AI breakthrough coming, what are the chances an outsider will?

Even worse, if you bought QQQ or even VOO back in 2000 (below shown SPY due to VOO's later inception date), you would face the "lost decade" between 2000-2010 if you haven't dollar-cost-averaged over this duration.

Yes, many make the argument dollar-cost-averaging would make your investment likely profitable within these 10 years, but who has the stomach to buy shares of an ETF, after it crashed 90%, as QQQ did between 2000-2002?

No Bargains In Sight

All of this brings me to valuation.

As you would guess, QQQ's valuation with a P/E of 31x is well above VOO's P/E of 22x, due to a mix of factors, such as concentration in a handful of growth juggernauts, without the exposure to more value-oriented parts of the economy, dominant exposure to asset-light businesses and overall exposure to higher margin/higher pricing power businesses.

Even if we look at the 12M forward basis, QQQ's P/E is 27x and VOO's is at 19x.

What this simply tells us, is that QQQ is more exposed to valuation compression if growth would disappoint, or if for instance interest rates would rise once again, which would in turn discount the appeal of future cashflows, or if momentum/AI hype fades.

On the other hand, VOO's valuation, even though not specifically attractive, is better supported by earnings across various sectors and isn't deviating too far out from historical averages.

Yet, I would say both ETFs are, to a certain degree, exposed to the risk of growth slowing/not delivering on expectations that could impact their valuations to the downside, as you see below from the historical context:

Timeline VOO P/E QQQ P/E
2015 Pre-Tech Boom ~18x ~22x
2021 COVID-19 Trading Frenzy ~24x ~38x
2022 Interest Rate Hikes & Tech Crash ~17x ~25x
Mid-2025 (Today) ~22x ~31x

So, To Own VOO Or QQQ?

With all that said, which one is a better choice for an investor with 20-30 years' time horizon?

We concluded that VOO's portfolio is simply more diversified across sectors, businesses, mega/mid/small-caps, all supporting its lower volatility, healthier valuation, but also contributing to lower long-term returns.

Conversely, QQQ's tech-heavy allocation is responsible for better risk-adjusted returns, but 50%+ exposure to its top 10 holdings, exposed to innovation and momentum stocks, with rich valuations, is responsible for deeper and longer-lasting corrections and drawdowns.

That's why I would say, it all comes down to whether one can stomach the extra volatility and has the opportunity to ride out lost decades, ideally dollar-cost-averaging.

If the answer is yes in both instances, QQQ has the edge to outperform and build significantly larger wealth compared to VOO.

On the other side, if one values a good night's sleep and stability, VOO fits the box.

In an ideal world, I would recommend for investors with a similar investment profile as mine, with long compounding durations ahead, to go 70% VOO and 30% QQQ to capture the full US economy, exposed to core sectors, but add the pinch of spice with QQQ, to give us the opportunity to deliver alpha over the S&P 500 benchmark.

data.title: Macquarie Limited-Term Diversified Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Economic growth in the US slowed further in 1Q25, continuing a trend that began in the second half of 2023. The slowdown became more apparent during the quarter, possibly reflecting consumer and business caution due to rising uncertainty around the Trump administration's policy agenda (both in terms of trade policies and government spending cuts). The US Federal Reserve (Fed) maintained its restrictive monetary policies, which have continued to weigh on growth, although fiscal policy has remained supportive. Consumer spending slowed somewhat, and while this followed robust spending in 4Q24, a cooling labor market could cause consumers to remain cautious in the months ahead. Beyond this, while tariffs are likely to weigh on US growth, a resumption in the Fed's current easing cycle, along with renewed fiscal stimulus, should provide a material positive offset for economic activity in the medium term.

On inflation, the Core Consumer Price Index (Core CPI) again saw a significant increase in January - repeating the start-of-year pattern of recent years - although this was followed by a more subdued reading in February. The Fed's preferred Core Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) inflation measure indicated that inflation has been better contained over recent months, although the February reading (in contrast to the CPI) was elevated. The more modest pace of demand, ongoing supply expansion, and a labor market that continues to soften should maintain the broader downward trend in inflation. Even so, the path towards the Fed target looks to be slower and bumpier than was earlier anticipated. Labor demand has continued to cool as hiring trends slow, and labor supply has continued to grow, even as immigration has declined. With the resulting better balance in the labor market, wage growth to continue to ease. While tariffs should boost prices in the coming months, we believe that is likely to have only a one-off impact, especially with most measures of inflation expectations remaining well anchored. Even this one-off impact will likely be tempered by the negative impact tariffs may have on both global and US activity.

Outside the US, euro-zone data continued to point toward subdued economic activity growth in that region, while Chinese growth seemed to benefit somewhat from the stimulus measures announced in 2024. In Europe, the German economic outlook remains concerning in the near term, with structural headwinds buffeting key industries including automobiles and chemicals. Further downside risks stem from the US administration's trade policies. While these all point to downside risks in coming months, we believe a substantially more supportive fiscal outlook will boost growth beyond this with significant government spending on defense and infrastructure now planned over the next few years. Given the degree of slack currently present in the German economy, we do not anticipate materially higher inflation. The European Central Bank (ECB), after cutting rates by 100 basis points in 2024, followed this up with additional 25 basis-point rate cuts in January and March (one basis point is a hundredth of a percentage point). An additional two-and-a-half 25 basis-point cuts over the remainder of 2025 are now priced into rates markets.

The sharp rise in German bond yields that we saw following the recent fiscal-spending announcement serves as a reminder that fiscal outlooks remain a potential cause for concern in many European countries. Europe also remains susceptible to geopolitical events that can affect energy security, with the Russia-Ukraine war continuing to be a potential source of inflationary concern, along with ongoing tension in the Middle East. While the stimulus measures announced by a range of Chinese policymakers over recent months are helping to stabilize activity in the near term, the structural headwinds facing the Chinese economy remain strong, especially the ongoing and deepseated problems in the property sector. In contrast to most other major central banks, economic conditions in Japan, especially wage and inflation outcomes, are pushing the Bank of Japan (BoJ) to further increase rates as it continues to move away from its previous negative-rate policies. After raising rates in January, the rates markets expect a further increase in the months ahead.

While the higher bond yields at the start of 2025 provided attractive entry levels, the subsequent decline in yields since then suggests a more cautious stance is warranted. We will continue to consider increasing duration as yields rise but also reduce exposure when the market looks rich. In addition, geopolitical considerations such as the new US administration's policies are increasingly affecting bond yields, and the response of other countries to these policies will also be important. On credit, while the risk aversion we saw in markets has caused spreads to widen somewhat, this comes from a historically tight starting point. While risks to growth have increased, the prospect of further policy support (both monetary and fiscal) is likely to benefit the medium-term growth outlook. Moreover, while spreads remain relatively tight despite the recent widening, all-in yields remain high relative to recent history and expectations of positive total returns should drive demand.

Within the Fund

For 1Q25, Macquarie Limited-Term Diversified Income Fund (MUTF:DTINX) Institutional Class shares underperformed the Fund's benchmark, the Bloomberg 1-3 Year US Government/Credit Index.

Key contributors to performance included:

  • Yield curve management
  • Allocations to commercial mortgage-backed securities and mortgage-backed securities

Key detractors from performance included:

  • An allocation to asset-backed securities (ABS)
  • An allocation to high yield corporates

Outlook

In 1Q25, investors shifted their focus to the potentially deleterious effects of Trump's policies, including tariff wars, federal layoffs, and spending cuts. As a result, markets pulled back from historically rich valuations. Consumer and business sentiment was noticeably weaker, even as hard data remained resilient. Clarity around the scope of potential changes would help assuage market concerns but that may take some time to emerge. Meanwhile, there appears to be some agreement between market participants and the Fed that there will likely be two further cuts this year. That said, the potential for unexpected developments in the economy and the Fed's reaction remains a concern for us.

Our strategy remains a defensive one, targeting attractive spread and yield advantage over the benchmark while maintaining high levels of liquidity. We tactically added some risk in the recent bout of market volatility but remain at the low end of risk historically. We have moved duration from a modest long to closer to home.

data.title: Macquarie Large Cap Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

It's hard to believe the DeepSeek event that caused a stir within the artificial intelligence realm occurred only last quarter. It feels like a lifetime ago. Since then, we experienced an onslaught of news and events that built additional risk into the economic and financial system, causing a shift in sentiment for US equities. The market generated negative returns over what was a volatile quarter and left many question marks in its wake.

Government policy and the opaque threat of tariffs left both corporations and consumers at a standstill. Consumer data showed signs of softening, with areas like consumer spending growth, auto loans, and credit card debt a potential concern. The greatest area of angst emerged from corporations. Many were paralyzed as they awaited what government policy will mean for their businesses. This eroded confidence, causing a slowdown in business spending and lowering earnings expectations for the coming quarters.

Big tech and AI-related stocks were hit hardest. These stocks had been experiencing outsized returns compared with the rest of the market as much of the investment community had been singularly focused on the narrative. The market broadened out as market leadership shifted to companies that had been all but forgotten over the prior 24 months. While masked by severe negative returns in the mega-cap companies, some sectors, such as consumer staples, industrials, and healthcare, generated positive returns during the quarter.

Risk factors played an important role in the market during the quarter but remain elevated. Beta, a measure of market risk, was down significantly. However, within large-cap growth equities, the index exposure to beta was still historically elevated. Another risk factor that did not yet correct was momentum. Given the scale of relative strength many of the high momentum stocks had, it may take time for that risk factor to unwind.

Elsewhere within financial markets, Treasury yields fell, the dollar weakened against a basket of major currencies, and oil was down slightly.

Within the Fund

For 1Q25, Macquarie Large Cap Growth Fund (MUTF:IYGIX) Institutional Class shares posted negative absolute returns but outperformed the Fund's benchmark, the Russell Growth Index. While the markets were challenged, the environment favored our style of investing on a relative basis as performance gravitated toward higher-quality businesses.

At a sector level, stock selection in financials, consumer discretionary, and information technology contributed most, along with an overweight allocation to the financials sector. Stock selection in communication services and healthcare detracted the most.

At the individual stock level, the greatest contribution was attributable to not owning Tesla (TSLA), and our positions in Intercontinental Exchange Inc. (ICE) and Visa Inc. (V) Tesla faced well-publicized headwinds last quarter that may bleed into future periods. This has remained a constant stock of debate among the investment community and is volatile as a result. The business has never met our quality standards and we are happy to sit on the sidelines of this battleground stock. ICE is a leading financial exchange operator, fixed income data provider, and mortgage technology company. While many know it as the owner of the New York Stock Exchange, the company is well diversified and essentially a monopoly across several critical businesses like energy futures trading, end-to-end digital mortgage origination, and fixed income pricing needed for exchange-traded funds. The stock recently fluctuated with interest rates but drove strong revenue growth in 2024 and should compound shareholder return over the long term through its diversified set of businesses. Visa, the US-based financial technology company, is consistently one of the most profitable business models we can invest in. The stock fell under pressure in 2024 as there were expectations for guidance revisions, volume growth slowed, and chatter heightened around anti-competitive practices. The core business may be more mature in the US but still grows at a healthy and durable pace. Meanwhile, the cash-to-card conversion is still underpenetrated internationally, and the company's Value Add Services segment is still immature and has a long runway for growth. The recent rerating in the stock was warranted, and we believe long-term trends are still quite favorable.

The largest individual detractors from performance relative to the benchmark were not owning Meta Platforms (META), not owning Eli Lilly & Co. (LLY), and our position in Electronic Arts Inc. (EA) Meta stock was slightly negative to end the quarter but relative to the benchmark and other communication services stock performed well. Having a zero weight relative to the large benchmark allocation hurt. We continue to follow this company closely and while we have become incrementally more constructive, we still have lingering concerns about the business model and worry the cyclical weakness in advertising spend could create further pressure. Eli Lilly stock recovered following a period of volatility in the second half of 2024 as investors digested the possibility of competition, sustained compliance for patients taking its weight-loss therapy, and side effects, among other threats that the valuation hadn't reflected. The stock recovered some value in 1Q25, but we continue to be on the sidelines. Lastly, Electronic Arts is one of the leading video game developers with a portfolio that includes franchises like Madden NFL, EA Sports FC, and The Sims. While the company transitioned to digital, a tailwind for margins, and maintained nearly impenetrable competitive positions across several popular genres, it also made some acquisition missteps into mobile and has been unable to further monetize its competitive strengths. The next catalyst for a stock rerating has been absent, and we became concerned the quality characteristics that made this business so compelling were declining. With that, we exited the position.

During the quarter, we exited two positions, including Electronic Arts, discussed above, and added four stocks to the portfolio. One of our new positions is in IDEXX Laboratories Inc. (IDXX), an animal health company with a primary focus on diagnostics for companion pets. The stock was weak over the past year as the COVID-19 pandemic pet boom and associated veterinary clinic visits normalized. The company is dominant in the space with a large installed base of its diagnostic equipment, which in turn drives the consumables business - a classic razor/razorblade model. As the large population of pets adopted during the pandemic age, there will be an increased need for vet services and testing. We took advantage of weakness in shares to start a position.

Outlook

It seems increasingly clear to us that stock market volatility is likely to persist in the intermediate term, driven by heightened uncertainty around economic growth, business confidence, and consumer health. This mix of limited visibility into key economic indicators creates a challenging investment environment, complicating decisions on risk-taking. Moreover, the market has been progressively extending risk exposure for some time now, providing a vulnerable starting point as conditions become more uncertain. Volatility doesn't necessarily mean a major market drawdown, but we believe forward market returns could be challenged.

These periods are good for stable high-quality growth businesses. Companies in which investors have a high degree of confidence that relatively strong fundamentals can be maintained tend to command a premium for that consistency. This should favor our style of investing.

As mentioned above, underlying risk factors remain elevated and are having an outsized negative impact on stocks that characterize these risks (i.e. momentum and beta). We have been underweight these factors, which was a drag on relative performance in 2023/2024 but should be a tailwind as it reverses.

History has shown that over entire market cycles the market favors companies with strong long-term fundamentals. Lower-quality beta and momentum-driven rallies can boom, but they are subject to volatility and sharp reversals. A quality approach has inherent advantages in the long run, and we continue to maintain that discipline. We believe we are in the early innings of realizing those advantages.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal.Market risk is the risk that all or a majority of the securities in a certain market - like the stock market or bond market - will decline in value because of factors such as adverse political or economic conditions, future expectations, investor confidence, or heavy institutional selling. - The prices of growth stocks may rise or fall dramatically and may be more volatile, particularly over the short term.

Because large-capitalization companies tend to be less volatile than companies with smaller capitalizations, the value of a fund or strategy that invests in large-capitalization companies may not rise as much as a fund or strategy that invests in smaller-capitalization companies. - A fund or strategy that holds a limited number of securities may be more sensitive to the price fluctuations of a single security in its portfolio and may be morevolatile than a fund or strategy that holds a greater number of securities. - Because a non-diversified fund or strategy may invest its assets in fewer issuers, the value of its shares may increase or decrease more rapidly than if it were fully diversified.

Issuers in the information technology sector may be impacted by intense competition; dramatic and often unpredictable changes in growth rates and competition for qualified personnel; effects on profitability from being heavily dependent on patent and intellectual property rights and the loss or impairment of those rights; obsolescence of existing technology; general economic conditions; and government regulation. - The value of securities in a particular industry or sector will decline because of changing expectations for the performance of that industry or sector. - The fund or strategy is subject to liquidity risk, which is the risk that the fund or strategy's investments cannot be readily sold within seven calendar days at approximately the price at which the fund or strategy has valued them.

Risk is increased in a concentrated portfolio since it holds a limited number of securities with each investment having a greater effect on the overall performance.
